item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What does "He was acutely aware of how much narrative fuel he had to burn, and tried to keep his sentences as long as possible." mean? (hidden in the page source)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Slapped so hard he died and became more like a caveman. Gold! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanna see what happens when you give it to a homeless person in an alleyway for free.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Plates are expensive, man. So are ingredients.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

SCP-1622 and SCP-759 are both similar in that in each article food seems to absorb personality which causes problems down the line. It could be that SCP-1622 is a very specific version of the phenomenon observed in SCP-759. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1622's anomalous effects, aside from the taste itself, center around its storage, preparation, and consumption, and will not manifest when the total cost of preparing a meal containing or consisting of SCP-1622 is greater than US$97,250. This includes the average cost of ingredients, all furnishings in the environment in which it is served, the utensils used in preparation and consumption, and the average restaurant price of the dish that it has been used in.1 Subjects consuming SCP-1622 in this fashion demonstrate no unusual behaviors aside from a marked dislike of other cheeses. This is believed to be due to the superior flavor of SCP-1622, and is not considered anomalous.

To this:

SCP-1622's anomalous effects, aside from the taste itself, center around its storage, preparation, and consumption, and will not manifest when the total cost of preparing a meal containing or consisting of SCP-1622 is greater than US$97,250. This includes the average cost of ingredients, all furnishings in the environment in which it is served, the utensils used in preparation and consumption, and the average restaurant price of the dish that it has been used in.2 Subjects consuming SCP-1622 in this fashion demonstrate no unusual behaviors aside from a marked dislike of other cheeses. This is believed to be due to the superior flavor of SCP-1622, and is not considered anomalous.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

Footnotes
1. It is unknown how SCP-1622 determines the average restaurant price of dishes, but it has so far been consistent with Foundation estimates.
2. It is unknown how SCP-1622 determines the average restaurant price of dishes, but it has so far been consistent with Foundation estimates. A hypothesis that SCP-1622 absorbs this information
from the environment in a manner similar to SCP-759 is currently under investigation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My favorite part about this cheese is that it's so snobby that it likes to be baked in a fruit tart. How pretentious.

Unless people do bake cheese into fruit tarts, I'm not as classy as this cheese honestly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Snobby cheese, snobby cheese

Upvoted, but it has some iffy writing, mostly in the Containment section.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw the total cost of the last test being $95 and I think "oh this ones a goner".

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't care, or rather, care too little about the parts that make no sense here. The WAIT WHA factor is too darn high to prevent an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With triceratops steaks and some toast from me, obviously. Otherwise, it's just not breakfast.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Guys.
Man eggs covered in whale cheese, with a side of clown milk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's two types of criminals who end up in jail: the dumb ones (like the Duct Tape Bandit) and the unlucky ones (like All Capone). The dumb ones get caught because they don't think things through. The unlucky ones get caught because they overlooked the details (that they may or may not know of) that gets them arrested and convicted.

It's not impossible to have geniuses on death row, because they were unlucky.

Oh, and Al Capone was unlucky because his spending habits clearly showed he had more money than he reported to the IRS. So, convicted of tax evasion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hannibal lecter is a genius, d-class come from death row… its not exactly streaching reality to assume that we've got quite a few genius d-class laying around, sometimes you have to be fairly smart to get away with serial murder

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some reason, I assumed this was a take on the whole "so good you'll slap your momma" thing. With the price deciding how far in time said 'slap' occurs, affecting your education and what not.

Or am I just powerfully insane? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They wanted somebody who could still talk to them when they had finished.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
IQ tests administered prior to test showed an average score of 125. Tests following consumption show an average score of 111.

Okay, I just looked, and nobody has brought up the fact that D-1622-1 is a genius. An IQ of 125 is just within that range. I mean, you don't have to lower it, because it kind of makes a little sense, but still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1: You broke the curse.

2: This is brilliant. Cheese slap!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whale cheese.

Whale.

Cheese.

Excuse me, I have to go come to terms with the unimaginable cosmic horror of this.

e: however, this deep-seated horror is based not on the article itself but rather eccentric headcanon implications. For the article itself, I no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pleased that you went with my suggestion, but I should point out that whale milk is so high in fat it's got a consistency thicker than toothpaste. Whale cheese would be… I'm not sure if it's safe for human consumption at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I would probably get hit so hard I would just be a splatter on the wall and floor. ):

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like I said, do you know how much it costs to buy a McDonald's restaurant? Far more than $97,000. Hell, a single pizza oven used at Domino's can cost over $50,000. -look here-Cost of equipment is often quite independent of cost of the meal… if not an inverse scale. A good chef can make a fabulous meal with nothing but pots and pans and a gas stove.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bravo. I don't know why exactly. But I like this one. You made whale cheese into an SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's going to be so incredibly fuzzy that it's impossible to adjust in anything other than vague increments. This isn't D&D, where price is an intrinsic part of things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It judges the average cost of the meal, the chairs, the carpets, the table, the silverware, and the lighting in the room it is served in, as well as any appliance directly involved in preparing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He claims its the "average cost of a meal." Where on God's green earth is the average cost of a meal going to be $97,000? You could eat 50 lbs of gold or 16 lbs of reactor-grade Plutonium for that price.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuckin' whale cheese, man. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The cheese is snobby, but not as snobby as this comment.

And yes, furnishings make a difference. No, it doesn't cost 97k$ for a McDonald's, but the cheese doesn't look at the whole building it's in to diagnose furnishings. It'd estimate the price cost of the tray and table it's on, maybe the seat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subjects consuming SCP-1622 in this fashion demonstrate no unusual behaviors aside from a marked dislike of other cheeses. This is believed to be due to the superior flavor of SCP-1622, and is not considered anomalous.

This sounds crazy anomalous to me. I would be very surprised if blue whale milk produces particularly tasty cheese, and in any event, a lot of a cheese's quality comes from its creation and treatment. It would be quite possible to ruin even a superb cheese with poor preparation. Moreover, there's no linear scale of cheeses. I like certain cheeses for munching, but they'd be terrible in, say, pizza. So I don't know why the article is treating the cheese's flavor like it's nonanomalous, when it's just as obviously anomalous as the slapping. Plus, I don't buy the ability to calculate the cost of preparing and presenting to the cent.

I get the idea (haughty cheese), but the presentation is too careless for me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Blue whale cheese in and of itself probably costs a pretty penny…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lower the price point to $97 instead of the retarded $97,000 and I will gladly upvote.

….

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lowering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral vote.

I would upvote (I love Cheese) but the arbitrary, ludicrously high price points are making it impossible for me to vote in good faith.

I mean $97,000? Look at this. There's no meal on that list at more than $1000 per person.

Okay, so its the cost of the furnishings? Okay, well it costs far more than $97,000 to buy or build a McDonald's.

Lower the price point to $97 instead of the retarded $97,000 and I will gladly upvote. And a cheeseburger at $56? I think you mean 56 cents or $0.56.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fine addition to the library of articles tagged "gustatory". Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first, I was partially entertained, but after reading that the cheese basically needs to be eaten in a more proper setting I was equally intrigued and amused. Definitely original.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm imagining some being somewhere going "YOU PHILISTINE!" every time there's a slap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No-voted. Although I like the idea of elitist cheese, there are some weird grammar issues and other logical issues going on.

Hella big wall of text
Plays hide and seek

Transportation to and from testing is to be carried out solely by D-class personnel, on a gilded platter of pure silver, covered by a dish cover of same.

This is a dangling modifier. Makes it sound like the D-class are on a gilded platter, covered with a silver dish cover.

All Balaenoptera musculus migration routes are to be monitored for unusual activity. Any persons attempting to harvest milk from a Balaenoptera musculus are to be interrogated as to their purpose and administered Class-B amnestics.

Most scientists track blue whales (or any whale for that matter) using satellite tags that are attached to the whales themselves According to NOAA, there are 5000-12000 blue whales in the world. How are you going to track each and every single one of them 24/7 to make sure that nobody's milking them? Moreover, satellite tags don't really relay any other information besides the position of the whale. You'd have to have a team that tracks every single blue whale in existence, all of the time in order to make sure that they're not being milked.

"perfect", and etc

You don't need "and" with "etc." The "et" in "et cetera" means "and."

the average restaurant price of the dish that it has been used in

My family has a background in the restaurant industry. There really are no "average prices," since the price of a single dish will vary depending on the quality of the restaurant.

Searches of the area yielded stores of SCP-1622 at all restaurants mentioned as well as three that had not.

Little weird language here. I know what you mean, but it took me a second try to understand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still though, that cheeseburger and a plate still costs 36 bucks somehow? That's a little overboard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From my understanding it's the whole room, silverware, plates, tiling, etc… costs that amount to a total of ~50.000 USD.
It's like if you eat this cheese in a fancy room you get less retarded than if you eat it in a mud hut.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
None of those collapsibles are necessary.

How does making it into a cheeseburger and putting it on a plate cost fifty grand?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They weren't intentional. Thanks for pointing it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Psst… you forgot to add a rating module.

I like it. Anomalous cheese that attacks anyone who eats it under the wrong circumstances. It's certainly an article with flavor.

One thing I noticed, in the first paragraph of the description:

and etc.

Seems a bit redundant.

Also, your brackets don't match up when you're redacting the superfluous logs, but that might have been intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still not sure about that recovery log… oh well.
